_vars.raw_problem: As a financial analyst for a mid-sized manufacturing company, you are tasked with evaluating an investment proposal to expand production capacity. The project involves purchasing new machinery and renovating an existing facility. You need to prepare a capital budgeting report that includes calculations of the Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) for the project. The proposal provides a cost estimate for the machinery but lacks specifics on the renovation costs and any potential changes in working capital. Additionally, assume a discount rate, but the basis for this rate is not provided. Your report should conclude with a recommendation on whether to proceed with the investment, considering both financial metrics and any strategic business factors you deem relevant. Present your findings to the senior management team.
_vars.student_framing: [{"section_type": "Problem Definition", "content": "Clearly define the investment proposal parameters, including the specific machinery to be purchased, the scope of renovations, and the expected increase in production capacity. Identify the time frame for the investment and the expected operational life of the new machinery."}, {"section_type": "Cost Estimation", "content": "Request detailed renovation cost estimates from relevant departments or contractors, including labor, materials, and any potential overhead costs. Clarify the expected changes in working capital, including inventory and receivables adjustments due to increased production."}, {"section_type": "Discount Rate Justification", "content": "Determine the appropriate discount rate for the NPV calculations by evaluating the company's weighted average cost of capital (WACC), considering the risk profile of the investment and market conditions. Provide a rationale for the chosen rate."}, {"section_type": "Financial Metrics Calculation", "content": "Outline the methodology for calculating NPV and IRR, including cash flow projections over the investment's life. Ensure to include all relevant cash inflows and outflows, such as tax implications and maintenance costs."}, {"section_type": "Strategic Analysis", "content": "Incorporate strategic business factors into the analysis, such as alignment with company goals, market demand forecasts, competitive positioning, and potential risks associated with the investment."}, {"section_type": "Recommendation Framework", "content": "Establish criteria for making the investment recommendation, weighing financial metrics against strategic considerations. Prepare to present alternative scenarios, such as best-case and worst-case outcomes, to provide a comprehensive view to senior management."}]

criteria.name: Problem Identification
criteria.description: Ability to identify the core components of the capital budgeting problem, including cash flows, discount rates, and time horizon.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Clearly identifies all relevant components and their interdependencies within the capital budgeting context.
criteria.poor_indicator: Fails to identify key components or misinterprets their relevance to the capital budgeting problem.

criteria.name: Assumption Articulation
criteria.description: Ability to articulate clear and relevant assumptions underlying the capital budgeting analysis.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Provides well-reasoned assumptions that align with financial principles and the specifics of the problem.
criteria.poor_indicator: Assumptions are vague, irrelevant, or contradict basic financial principles.

criteria.name: Task Definition
criteria.description: Ability to translate the ill-defined problem into a well-defined task with clear objectives and constraints.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Formulates a specific, actionable task that is logically structured and considers all given constraints.
criteria.poor_indicator: The task is vague, lacks clear objectives, or ignores key constraints.

typical_errors: Overlooking the impact of discount rates on cash flow valuation.
Misinterpreting the time horizon for the investment.
Ignoring inflation effects on future cash flows.

best_framing.base: As a financial analyst for a mid-sized manufacturing company, you are tasked with evaluating an investment proposal to expand production capacity. The project involves purchasing new machinery and renovating an existing facility. You need to prepare a capital budgeting report that includes calculations of the Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) for the project. The proposal provides a cost estimate for the machinery but lacks specifics on the renovation costs and any potential changes in working capital. Additionally, assume a discount rate, but the basis for this rate is not provided. Your report should conclude with a recommendation on whether to proceed with the investment, considering both financial metrics and any strategic business factors you deem relevant. Present your findings to the senior management team.

entries.title: Renovation Cost Estimate
entries.description: Assume that the renovation costs are estimated to be 30% of the machinery cost, based on industry standards for similar projects. This assumption is necessary due to the lack of specific renovation cost details in the proposal.

entries.title: Working Capital Changes
entries.description: Assume an increase in working capital equivalent to 10% of the initial machinery cost, reflecting typical increases in inventory and receivables associated with expanded production capacity.
entries.addresses_gap: Changes in working capital are not detailed

entries.title: Discount Rate Basis
entries.description: Assume a discount rate of 8%, derived from the company's weighted average cost of capital (WACC) based on historical data and comparable industry rates.
entries.addresses_gap: Basis for the discount rate is not provided

entries.title: Time Horizon
entries.description: The project evaluation should consider a 10-year time horizon, typical for capital investments in manufacturing, to capture long-term benefits and costs.
entries.addresses_gap: Misinterpreting the time horizon for the investment

entries.title: Financial Metrics
entries.description: The project's success will be primarily measured by achieving a positive NPV and an IRR that exceeds the assumed discount rate of 8%.
entries.addresses_gap: Overlooking the impact of discount rates on cash flow valuation

entries.title: Strategic Alignment
entries.description: Evaluate the project's alignment with the company's strategic goals, such as market expansion or technological leadership, as a secondary success metric.

entries.title: Definition of Strategic Business Factors
entries.description: Strategic business factors refer to non-financial considerations such as market positioning, competitive advantage, and alignment with long-term corporate strategy.
entries.addresses_gap: Ambiguity in what constitutes strategic business factors

best_framing.coverage_notes: This framing addresses all rubric criteria by clearly identifying problem components, articulating assumptions, and defining tasks with constraints. It ensures comprehensive coverage of financial principles and strategic considerations.

criticism: The student's framing captures several key aspects of the problem, such as the need for detailed cost estimation and the justification of the discount rate. However, it lacks explicit articulation of assumptions, particularly regarding the renovation costs and changes in working capital. The framing could have been more explicit in detailing these assumptions to ensure clarity. Additionally, while the student mentions strategic analysis, the specifics of how strategic factors will be integrated into the financial analysis are not thoroughly described.
better_alternative: A stronger framing would include a more explicit articulation of assumptions, such as specifying assumed percentages for renovation costs and working capital changes based on industry standards. It would also clearly outline how strategic business factors will be quantitatively and qualitatively assessed alongside financial metrics, ensuring a balanced analysis that considers both financial and strategic dimensions.

per_criterion_scores.internal_note: Identified most components but lacked clarity on interdependencies and specifics of working capital changes.
Assumptions were implied but not clearly articulated or justified, particularly for renovation costs and working capital.
The task was generally well-defined, but lacked some specificity in integrating strategic factors into the financial analysis.

strengths: The student correctly identified the need for detailed cost estimation and justification of the discount rate.
The framing included a strategic analysis component, acknowledging the importance of non-financial factors.
refinements_missed: Explicit integration of strategic factors into financial analysis
